Patch Set 8:

Alex, there was nothing wrong was the local list. But the code looked cleaner with an additional ref. Also I think it's safer that way as well - e.g. if it were written that way from the beginning, then I probably wouldn't have made the mistake of using th/oh in the first place.

I can change it back to using local list if you'd like. But I wonder, if thandle_put() is removed, then how does osd-zfs know when to stop using oh/tx? If, for example, it'd be guaranteed to be available before stop_trans() returns, then we wouldn't need a local list either.

So I'd tend to stay with the current version and handle it when thandle_put() is being removed. There's other uses of thandle_put() in osd-zfs as well, so it's not adding additional complexity to future removal of thandle_put().

Patch Set 8:

Two sanity-lfsck failures for patch set 8:
- One with ldiskfs, so that's irrelevant.
- The other one was caused by LU-6272.

Patch Set 8:

Isaac, thandle is supposed to be used by an internal-to-OSD commit callback. or, if commit cb isn't required, then by ->dt_trans_stop() immediately.
your patch brings additional work for Di as his patchset removes thandle_put() thing.
yet another thought was that potentially we can be calling few ->do_declare_destroy()  concurrently. can't we make the decision in ->do_destroy() ? probably mark the object for delayed destroy at declaration, but put on the list at execution?
Uploaded patch set 9.

Patch Set 9:

Two sanity test_160a time out reported to LU-6144.

Patch Set 9:

Isaac, have you seen my comment about declare vs. declare race?

Patch Set 9:

Did you mean that do_declare_destroy() can be called concurrently on a same oh/tx? If so, the oh->ot_unlinked_list is already protected by oh->ot_lists_lock so that's not a problem.

But I vaguely recall that the DMU layer assumes there's no concurrent access on a same TX. E.g. dmu_tx_hold_write()->dmu_tx_hold_object_impl():
list_insert_tail(&tx->tx_holds, txh);

I didn't see any serialization on the tx->tx_holds. We may have a bigger potential problem if that do_declare_destroy() concurrency is expected.

As to delaying decision to do_destroy(), the DMU tx is already assigned, so there can't by any further dmu_tx_hold* calls.

Patch Set 9:

no, I mean few different threads (so txes) can call ->do_declare_destroy() concurrently.

Patch Set 9:

Patch Set 9:

So different threads would try do_declare_destroy() a same object (though from different oh/tx)? Does the same also happen to do_destroy()?

Patch Set 9:

I'd say we should allow this, internal consistency should be maintained by OSD - any additional requirement (like external locking) isn't good. but I don't think this can happen now.

Patch Set 9: Verified-1

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.6/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/4c5e8c98-c34f-11e4-b384-5254006e85c2).  Ran 17 tests.  1 tests failed: sanity-lfsck.

Patch Set 9:

Just to double check, you were saying the following situations can't happen now, but the OSD code should be able to handle it any way:
- do_declare_destroy() can be called concurrently for a same object, though with different th/oh/tx.
- same thing for do_destroy().

Would it also indicate that do_declare_destroy() and do_destroy() could happen concurrently on a same object as well?

Patch Set 9:

sorry if I'm not clear (or made a mistake):
- concurrent declaration is possible now
- concurrent destroy shouldn't happen now
- ideally we should allow concurrent destroy (just to make API simple/clean/consistent)

Patch Set 9:

racing declare vs destroy is possible

Patch Set 9:

How about:
1. Add a field to struct osd_object: struct osd_thandle *oo_destroy_tx;
2. In declare_destroy(), set obj->oo_destroy_tx = oh if it was NULL. Only one oh would win, and everything else just returns without doing anything (or maybe return a -EBUSY in this case?).
3. In destroy(), do nothing if obj->oo_destroy_tx != oh.
4. In trans_stop(), clear obj->oo_destroy_tx if it equals oh, in case the destroy fails and the object would need to be destroyed again.

So only one th/oh/tx would do the actual declare and destroy. Everything else if any would just check obj->oo_destroy_tx and do nothing.

Patch Set 9:

hmm, actual race is happening at destroy (which should be serialized externally or internally), by that time we don't know which tx is really going to win. even more - declare isn't a promise to execution, it's just a declaration of possible change. IOW, every tx has to declare properly, but only one will do actual change.
I was thinking of a flag in osd_object which marks object as a subject to delayed destroy, then any subsequent declaration/destroy has to follow delayed destroy.
Uploaded patch set 10.

Patch Set 10:

I assumed obj->oo_attr.la_size doesn't change for an obj to be destroyed, and used it to test if to delay destroy. osd_object_unlinked_add() is now used to serialize destroy().

(1 comment)

from the API's point of view there is nothing preventing write vs. destroy race. IMO, it'd be good if we don't require external locking to have this working. though it should work fine now. but if in the future the race becomes possible it'll be very hard to catch it.

Patch Set 10:

(1 comment)

Andreas commented on the ticket "Note that by the time that the OST object is being destroyed it has already been unlinked from the MDS namespace, and that unlink is committed on the MDS. The OFD has also revoked client locks and discarded any cached dirty pages on the clients, so there is no danger to destroy the object in stages."

So I thought there'd be no write vs destroy race. If that's still possible, it'd be hard to handle objects growing in size while destroy in progress:
1. declare_destroy() decides to sync free
2. New writes make the object grow very large
3. Then destroy() will have to check size before sync free, and it can't be sync freed any more, fail the trans.

Patch Set 10:

(1 comment)

unlink on MDT (even committed) can not guarantee no writes in flight from an evicted client, for example. OST_DESTROY invalidates LDLM locks, so regular OST_WRITE can't race, but OST_WRITE still can.  a trivial workaround (not that I like it) is to apply this logic depending on sequence type or OSD type: MDT or OST.

Patch Set 10:

please read "but lockless OST_WRITE still can race"

Patch Set 10:

Thanks Alex! I'll update the patch.

parents.subject: LU-5975 quota: remove obsolete quota code

commit.subject: LU-5242 osd-zfs: umount hang in sanity 133g
commit.message:
LU-5242 osd-zfs: umount hang in sanity 133g

It's not optimal to free large/sparse object directly by
dmu_object_free(). The dmu_tx_count_free() may over estimate the
memory overhead and cause the TX to fail in arc_tempreserve_space()
and eventually hang.

The correct way is to truncate the object with dmu_free_long_range()
before freeing it. This patch moves large object to ZPL unlinked set
and then later truncates and frees it, with as many TXs as needed.

Change-Id: I077dbcafff5abaea4a42dc9bc3f9ae980bdcdc54
Signed-off-by: Isaac Huang <he.huang@intel.com>
Test-Parameters: envdefinitions=SLOW=yes mdtfilesystemtype=zfs mdsfilesystemtype=zfs ostfilesystemtype=zfs
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/13630
Tested-by: Jenkins
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
